name: production-incident-playbook
description: End-to-end incident handling — runbook templates for in-flight mitigation (detect / triage / mitigate / resolve / communicate) + blameless postmortem templates for after-action review with 5-whys root-cause + structural action items. Use during active incidents or post-incident for the postmortem write-up. Distinct from security-incident-response (security-specific incidents) and pre-ship-gate (pre-deploy catching).

positive_triggers.input: Alert just fired — production error rate is 5x baseline. Walk me through what to do in the next 30 minutes.
positive_triggers.reason: Active incident in-flight → matches Use when 'active production incident: alert fired, you're the on-call or named incident commander'

positive_triggers.input: We had a SEV1 last week — outage for 47 minutes. Need to write the postmortem this week before the on-call rotation shifts.
positive_triggers.reason: Postmortem within 5-day window → matches Use when 'postmortem time: 1-5 days since incident'

positive_triggers.input: Build me a runbook template for our checkout service so the next on-call has a checklist.
positive_triggers.reason: Proactive runbook template design → matches Use when 'writing a runbook template for a service / surface'

negative_triggers.input: API key leaked on a public repo; what's the incident response?
negative_triggers.reason: Security-specific incident (leaked credential) → matches NOT for 'security incidents' → security-incident-response routes here

negative_triggers.input: Just deployed, want to confirm it's not silently failing
negative_triggers.reason: Pre-deploy catching of silent failures → matches NOT for 'pre-deploy catching' → pre-ship-gate routes here

behavioral_evals.scenario: On-call engineer receives an alert at 02:00 — production p99 is 4× baseline, error rate climbing. They reach for production-incident-playbook.
expected_behavior: Phase 1 (Detect): confirm alert is real (not noisy / test / staging); if false positive, silence + file follow-up
Phase 2 (Triage): within 5 min, fill incident header (started / detected / severity / affected surface / customer-visible / blameless)
Phase 3 (Mitigate): within 30 min for SEV1/2 — apply rollback or feature-flag-OFF or rate-limit, document in incident channel
Phase 4 (Resolve): root cause via debugging-and-error-recovery (5-step triage) + diagnosing-bugs (symptom-driven loop) — fix deployed via normal pre-ship-gate path
Phase 5 (Communicate): status updates every 30-60 min in incident channel; status page update within 30 min of customer-visible incidents
Phase 6 (Close): confirm customer impact gone; schedule postmortem within 5 business days; 'all clear' in channel
Within 5 business days: postmortem with summary, timeline (precise UTC), 5-whys root cause, action items (concrete owner + date + type), blameless tone

behavioral_evals.scenario: Engineer is asked to write a postmortem for an incident from last week. The skill helps them structure it.
expected_behavior: Skill surfaces the postmortem template (§ Part 2)
Summary in customer-impact terms (1 paragraph) — not internal jargon
Root cause via 5 whys — keeps asking until structural cause reached (not 'Alice forgot', but 'no check for connection-pool budget in review template')
What went well / what went poorly / where we got lucky — honest sections, not sugar-coated
Action items with concrete owner + due date + type — no 'improve monitoring' without an owner
Review-stage-2 (OMO review-work or equivalent) before publishing to org

notes: Merges antigravity's incident-runbook-templates + postmortem-writing into one workflow with explicit in-flight vs after-action phases. Distinct from security-incident-response (security-specific, NIST CSF simplified). Distinct from pre-ship-gate (pre-deploy catching). 5-day postmortem deadline is generous; SEV1/2 must hit it. Action items with concrete owners + dates are the lever — postmortems without action items are theatre. Quarterly review ensures action items < 90 days old get attention; > 90 days old with no progress escalate.
